skill.name: aws-cost-cleanup
skill.description: Find, review, and clean up unused AWS resources with dry-run workflows and cost-saving guidance.
skill.summary: Guides AWS teams through cost cleanup discovery, review, and controlled deletion workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell, AWS CLI, boto3, and blocker-keyword patterns. Review found no evidence of malware, credential theft, command-and-control behavior, or prompt injection; the blocker and weak-crypto detections are false positives from AWS cleanup terminology and code examples. The skill still carries medium operational risk because it includes destructive AWS cleanup examples, including an automated Lambda path that deletes unattached EBS volumes.

medium_findings.title: Destructive AWS Cleanup Examples Require Human Review
medium_findings.description: The skill includes AWS CLI and boto3 examples for deleting EBS volumes, deleting snapshots, releasing Elastic IPs, and an automated Lambda that deletes unattached volumes older than seven days. These are legitimate cost-cleanup actions, but publishing should warn users to test, review ownership, and require approvals before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly show destructive AWS cleanup operations and safety controls. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the surrounding workflow repeatedly instructs dry-run, validation, stakeholder notification, and rollback planning.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Contextual Examples
low_findings.description: The scanner correctly detected shell snippets, command substitution, AWS CLI commands, and Kiro CLI examples. They are presented as user-run examples for AWS operations, not hidden execution by the skill itself; however, users could cause resource changes if they copy and run them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible inside Markdown code fences and align with the skill purpose. I found no evidence that the skill executes them automatically or hides an injection path.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to AWS documentation and blog resources. I found no network request code or data exfiltration behavior associated with these links.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain Markdown links to AWS-owned documentation pages. No fetch, curl, webhook, credential upload, or external callback code is present in this context.

low_findings.title: Malware Keyword and Weak-Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity static blocker findings appear to match benign AWS cleanup terms, resource discovery commands, and words such as snapshot or describe. I found no command-and-control instructions, cryptographic routines, obfuscation, persistence, or prompt-injection text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited contexts shows normal AWS inventory and cleanup language. No evidence found for malicious C2 behavior, weak cryptographic implementation, or system-compromise intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive AWS Cleanup Examples Require Human Review
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes AWS CLI and boto3 examples for deleting EBS volumes, deleting snapshots, releasing Elastic IPs, and an automated Lambda that deletes unattached volumes older than seven days. These are legitimate cost-cleanup actions, but publishing should warn users to test, review ownership, and require approvals before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly show destructive AWS cleanup operations and safety controls. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the surrounding workflow repeatedly instructs dry-run, validation, stakeholder notification, and rollback planning.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Detections Are Contextual Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The scanner correctly detected shell snippets, command substitution, AWS CLI commands, and Kiro CLI examples. They are presented as user-run examples for AWS operations, not hidden execution by the skill itself; however, users could cause resource changes if they copy and run them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible inside Markdown code fences and align with the skill purpose. I found no evidence that the skill executes them automatically or hides an injection path.

content.value_statement: Unused AWS resources create avoidable cloud spend and can be hard to review manually. This skill helps teams identify cleanup candidates, estimate savings, and plan controlled removal with safety checks.
actual_capabilities: Identifies cleanup targets such as unattached EBS volumes, old snapshots, unused Elastic IPs, and orphaned networking resources.
Provides AWS CLI examples for discovery, dry-run review, lifecycle policy setup, and cost anomaly alarms.
Shows a Python cost calculator for estimating monthly and annual savings from unused EBS volumes and Elastic IPs.
Includes a sample Lambda workflow for automated cleanup of aged unattached EBS volumes.
Defines a cleanup process with discovery, validation, execution, verification, and audit documentation steps.
Lists safety controls for backups, ownership checks, stakeholder notification, rollback planning, and restore testing.
limitations: It does not know whether a resource is business-critical without user-provided context and tags.
It cannot guarantee safe deletion for production accounts without human approval and dependency checks.
Cost estimates use simple example rates and may differ from current AWS pricing or regional charges.
Automation examples require properly scoped AWS credentials, IAM permissions, and account-specific testing.

use_cases.title: Monthly Cloud Waste Review
use_cases.description: Generate a candidate list of idle resources and estimate savings before a monthly optimization review.

use_cases.title: Safe Cleanup Runbook
use_cases.description: Create a step-by-step cleanup plan that includes dry-run checks, ownership validation, backups, and rollback notes.

use_cases.title: Account Cleanup Automation
use_cases.description: Adapt the provided AWS CLI and Lambda examples into controlled automation for non-production or approved accounts.

prompt_templates.title: Find Cleanup Candidates
prompt_templates.prompt: Use aws-cost-cleanup to list unused AWS resources I should review. Focus on EBS volumes, snapshots, Elastic IPs, and load balancers. Do not delete anything.

prompt_templates.title: Estimate Savings
prompt_templates.prompt: Use aws-cost-cleanup to create a savings estimate for idle EBS volumes and unused Elastic IPs. Include assumptions and mark values that need verification.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Safe Runbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Use aws-cost-cleanup to draft a cleanup runbook for my AWS account. Include discovery, owner review, backup checks, approval, execution, verification, and rollback steps.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Multi-Account Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use aws-cost-cleanup to design a multi-account cleanup workflow for AWS Organizations. Include IAM scope, dry-run reporting, tagging rules, approval gates, logging, and CloudWatch monitoring.

output_examples.input: Find unused AWS resources and estimate savings for a development account.
output_examples.output: A cleanup report that groups candidate EBS volumes, old snapshots, unused Elastic IPs, and estimated monthly savings. Each item is marked for owner review before deletion.

output_examples.input: Create a safe process to remove old snapshots.
output_examples.output: A runbook with cutoff criteria, compliance checks, owner notification, backup confirmation, approval steps, execution timing, and post-cleanup verification.

output_examples.input: Set up recurring cost cleanup monitoring.
output_examples.output: An automation plan that combines scheduled discovery, anomaly alerts, review reports, audit logging, and manual approval before any destructive action.

best_practices: Run discovery and dry-run steps before any deletion or release action.
Use tags, owners, dependency checks, and stakeholder approval to confirm resources are unused.
Keep backups, audit logs, and rollback plans for every cleanup campaign.
anti_patterns: Do not run deletion commands directly in production without review and approval.
Do not assume old snapshots or unattached volumes are safe to remove without compliance checks.
Do not use broad IAM permissions when a scoped cleanup role is sufficient.

faq.question: Can this skill delete AWS resources automatically?
faq.answer: It provides examples that can delete resources if a user enables and runs them. Review and approval are required before execution.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for production accounts?
faq.answer: Use it carefully in production. Start with discovery only, validate ownership, confirm backups, and require approval for destructive actions.

faq.question: What AWS resources does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on EBS volumes, EBS snapshots, S3 lifecycle cleanup, Elastic IPs, load balancers, NAT gateways, and network interfaces.

faq.question: Does it provide exact cost savings?
faq.answer: It provides estimate patterns. Actual savings depend on AWS region, pricing, resource type, storage class, and account discounts.

faq.question: Does it require AWS credentials?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples require AWS CLI or boto3 credentials with permissions for discovery and any approved cleanup actions.

faq.question: Can it work across multiple AWS accounts?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes an AWS Organizations pattern, but teams must configure profiles, roles, permissions, logging, and approvals.
